mn18:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling among the Sakyans at Kapilavatthu, in Nigrodha’s Park.
mn18:10.1: Then, not long after the *Bhagavā* had left, this occurred to those *bhikkhus*:
mn18:10.10: Venerable Mahākaccāna is capable of analyzing in detail the meaning of this brief statement set out by the *Bhagavā* without the meaning having been analyzed in detail.
mn18:10.11: What if we were to approach venerable Mahākaccāna and ask him about this matter?”
mn18:10.2: “The *Bhagavā* set out a brief statement for us without analyzing the meaning in detail, and then rose from his seat and entered his dwelling:
mn18:10.3: ‘From whatever source, *bhikkhu*, proliferating *saññā*-constructions arise in a person.
mn18:10.4: If there is nothing there to be delighted in, welcomed, or clung to.
mn18:10.5: This is the end of the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayānaṁ*] …
mn18:10.6: it is here that these evil, unwholesome *dhammas* cease without remainder.’
mn18:10.7: Now who could analyze in detail the meaning of this brief statement set out by the *Bhagavā* without the meaning having been analyzed in detail?”
mn18:10.9: “This venerable Mahākaccāna is praised by the Teacher and esteemed by discerning spiritual companions.
mn18:11.1: Then those *bhikkhus* approached venerable Mahākaccāna, and on arrival they exchanged greetings with him.
mn18:11.10: “From whatever source, *bhikkhu*, proliferating *saññā*-constructions arise in a person.
mn18:11.11: If there is nothing there to be delighted in, welcomed, or clung to.
mn18:11.12: This is the end of the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayānaṁ*] …
mn18:11.13: it is here that these evil, unwholesome *dhammas* cease without remainder.”’
mn18:11.14: Now who could analyze in detail the meaning of this brief statement set out by the *Bhagavā* without the meaning having been analyzed in detail?
mn18:11.16: ‘This venerable Mahākaccāna is praised by the Teacher and esteemed by discerning spiritual companions, and venerable Mahākaccāna is capable of analyzing in detail the meaning of this brief statement set out by the *Bhagavā* without the meaning having been analyzed in detail.
mn18:11.17: What if we were to approach venerable Mahākaccāna and ask him about this matter?’
mn18:11.2: After exchanging courteous and memorable talk, they sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, those *bhikkhus* said this to venerable Mahākaccāna:
mn18:11.3: “The *Bhagavā* set out a brief statement for us, friend Kaccāna, without analyzing the meaning in detail, and then rose from his seat and entered his dwelling:
mn18:11.4: ‘From whatever source, *bhikkhu*, proliferating *saññā*-constructions arise in a person.
mn18:11.5: If there is nothing there to be delighted in, welcomed, or clung to.
mn18:11.6: This is the end of the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayānaṁ*] …
mn18:11.7: it is here that these evil, unwholesome *dhammas* cease without remainder.’
mn18:11.8: Then, not long after the *Bhagavā* had left, this occurred to us:
mn18:11.9: ‘The *Bhagavā* set out a brief statement for us without analyzing the meaning in detail, and then rose from his seat and entered his dwelling:
mn18:12.1: “Friends, suppose a man in need of heartwood, seeking heartwood, wandering in search of heartwood, might pass right by the root and pass right by the trunk of a great standing tree possessed of heartwood, and think that heartwood should be sought among its branches and leaves.
mn18:12.2: So too, with the Teacher present face to face, you think this matter should be asked of us, passing over that *Bhagavā*.
mn18:12.3: For, friends, the *Bhagavā* knows by knowing, sees by seeing; he is one become vision, become knowledge, become *dhamma*, become Brahmā; he is the speaker, the proclaimer, the one who draws out the meaning, the giver of the deathless, the master of *dhamma*, the Tathāgata.
mn18:12.4: And that was the time when you should have asked this matter of the *Bhagavā* himself.
mn18:12.5: As the *Bhagavā* explained it to you, so should you remember it.”
mn18:13.1: “Certainly, friend Kaccāna, the *Bhagavā* knows by knowing, sees by seeing; he is one become vision, become knowledge, become *dhamma*, become Brahmā; he is the speaker, the proclaimer, the one who draws out the meaning, the giver of the deathless, the master of *dhamma*, the Tathāgata.
mn18:13.2: And that was the time when we should have asked this matter of the *Bhagavā* himself.
mn18:13.3: As the *Bhagavā* explained it to us, so would we remember it.
mn18:13.4: But venerable Mahākaccāna is praised by the Teacher and esteemed by discerning spiritual companions,
mn18:13.5: and venerable Mahākaccāna is capable of analyzing in detail the meaning of this brief statement set out by the *Bhagavā* without the meaning having been analyzed in detail.
mn18:13.6: May venerable Mahākaccāna analyze it without making it burdensome.”
mn18:14.1: “Then, friends, listen and attend carefully. I will speak.”
mn18:14.2: “Yes, friend,” those *bhikkhus* replied to venerable Mahākaccāna.
mn18:15.1: “What the *Bhagavā* set out for us in a brief statement, friends, without analyzing the meaning in detail, and then rose from his seat and entered his dwelling:
mn18:15.2: ‘From whatever source, *bhikkhu*, proliferating *saññā*-constructions arise in a person.
mn18:15.3: If there is nothing there to be delighted in, welcomed, or clung to, this is the end of the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayānaṁ*] …
mn18:15.4: it is here that these evil, unwholesome *dhammas* cease without remainder’—this is how I understand in detail the meaning of that brief statement set out by the *Bhagavā* without the meaning having been analyzed in detail:
mn18:16.1: Dependent on the eye, friends, and forms, eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] arises. The meeting of the three is contact. With contact as condition, *vedanā*. What one feels, that one perceives. What one perceives, that one thinks about [*vitakketi*]. What one thinks about [*vitakketi*], that one proliferates. What one proliferates, from that source proliferating *saññā*-constructions arise in a person with regard to forms cognizable by the eye — past, future, and present.
mn18:16.2: Dependent on the ear, friends, and sounds, ear-consciousness [*sotaviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn18:16.3: dependent on the nose, friends, and smells, nose-consciousness [*ghānaviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn18:16.4: dependent on the tongue, friends, and tastes, tongue-consciousness [*jivhāviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn18:16.5: dependent on the body, friends, and tangible things, body-consciousness [*kāyaviññāṇaṁ*] arises …
mn18:16.6: dependent on the mind, friends, and *dhammas*, mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] arises. The meeting of the three is contact. With contact as condition, *vedanā*. What one feels, that one perceives. What one perceives, that one thinks about [*vitakketi*]. What one thinks about [*vitakketi*], that one proliferates. What one proliferates, from that source proliferating *saññā*-constructions arise in a person with regard to *dhammas* cognizable by the mind — past, future, and present.
mn18:17.1: Now, friends, when eye is present, forms are present, and eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇe*] is present, then there could be a designation of contact — this possibility exists.
mn18:17.10: when mind is present, *dhammas* are present, and mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇe*] is present, then there could be a designation of contact — this possibility exists.
mn18:17.11: When a designation of contact is present, then there could be a designation of *vedanā* — this possibility exists.
mn18:17.12: When a designation of *vedanā* is present, then there could be a designation of *saññā* — this possibility exists.
mn18:17.13: When a designation of *saññā* is present, then there could be a designation of thought [*vitakka*] — this possibility exists.
mn18:17.14: When a designation of thought [*vitakka*] is present, then there could be a designation of the arising of proliferating *saññā*-constructions — this possibility exists.
mn18:17.2: When a designation of contact is present, then there could be a designation of *vedanā* — this possibility exists.
mn18:17.3: When a designation of *vedanā* is present, then there could be a designation of *saññā* — this possibility exists.
mn18:17.4: When a designation of *saññā* is present, then there could be a designation of thought [*vitakka*] — this possibility exists.
mn18:17.5: When a designation of thought [*vitakka*] is present, then there could be a designation of the arising of proliferating *saññā*-constructions — this possibility exists.
mn18:17.6: Now, friends, when ear is present and sounds are present …
mn18:17.7: when nose is present and smells are present …
mn18:17.8: when tongue is present and tastes are present …
mn18:17.9: when body is present and tangible things are present …
mn18:18.1: Now, friends, when eye is absent, forms are absent, and eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇe*] is absent, then there could be a designation of contact — this possibility does not exist.
mn18:18.10: when mind is absent, *dhammas* are absent, and mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇe*] is absent, then there could be a designation of contact — this possibility does not exist.
mn18:18.11: When a designation of contact is absent, then there could be a designation of *vedanā* — this possibility does not exist.
mn18:18.12: When a designation of *vedanā* is absent, then there could be a designation of *saññā* — this possibility does not exist.
mn18:18.13: When a designation of *saññā* is absent, then there could be a designation of thought [*vitakka*] — this possibility does not exist.
mn18:18.14: When a designation of thought [*vitakka*] is absent, then there could be a designation of the arising of proliferating *saññā*-constructions — this possibility does not exist.
mn18:18.2: When a designation of contact is absent, then there could be a designation of *vedanā* — this possibility does not exist.
mn18:18.3: When a designation of *vedanā* is absent, then there could be a designation of *saññā* — this possibility does not exist.
mn18:18.4: When a designation of *saññā* is absent, then there could be a designation of thought [*vitakka*] — this possibility does not exist.
mn18:18.5: When a designation of thought [*vitakka*] is absent, then there could be a designation of the arising of proliferating *saññā*-constructions — this possibility does not exist.
mn18:18.6: Now, friends, when ear is absent and sounds are absent …
mn18:18.7: when nose is absent and smells are absent …
mn18:18.8: when tongue is absent and tastes are absent …
mn18:18.9: when body is absent and tangible things are absent …
mn18:19.1: What the *Bhagavā* set out for us in a brief statement, friends, without analyzing the meaning in detail, and then rose from his seat and entered his dwelling:
mn18:19.2: ‘From whatever source, *bhikkhu*, proliferating *saññā*-constructions arise in a person, if there is nothing there to be delighted in, welcomed, or clung to, this is the end of the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayānaṁ*] …
mn18:19.3: it is here that these evil, unwholesome *dhammas* cease without remainder’—this is how I understand in detail the meaning of that brief statement set out by the *Bhagavā* without the meaning having been analyzed in detail.
mn18:19.4: But if you wish, venerable ones, you should approach the *Bhagavā* himself and ask him about this matter.
mn18:19.5: As the *Bhagavā* explains it to you, so should you remember it.”
mn18:2.1: Then the *Bhagavā*, in the morning, having robed up and taken bowl and robe, entered Kapilavatthu for alms.
mn18:2.2: After walking for alms in Kapilavatthu, after the meal, on returning from the almsround, he approached the Great Wood for the day’s abiding.
mn18:2.3: Having plunged into the Great Wood, he sat down for the day’s abiding at the root of a bael sapling.
mn18:20.1: Then those *bhikkhus*, having delighted in and approved of what venerable Mahākaccāna said, rose from their seats and approached the *Bhagavā*. On arrival, having bowed to the *Bhagavā*, they sat down to one side. Sitting to one side, those *bhikkhus* said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn18:20.10: If there is nothing there to be delighted in, welcomed, or clung to.
mn18:20.11: This is the end of the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayānaṁ*], this is the end of the underlying tendency to aversion [*paṭighānusayānaṁ*], this is the end of the underlying tendency to views [*diṭṭhānusayānaṁ*], this is the end of the underlying tendency to doubt [*vicikicchānusayānaṁ*], this is the end of the underlying tendency to conceit [*mānānusayānaṁ*], this is the end of the underlying tendency to lust for *bhava* [*bhavarāgānusayānaṁ*], this is the end of the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjānusayānaṁ*], this is the end of taking up rods and weapons, quarrels, conflicts, disputes, mutual accusations, divisive speech, and false speech.
mn18:20.12: It is here that these evil, unwholesome *dhammas* cease without remainder.”
mn18:20.13: Now who could analyze in detail the meaning of this brief statement set out by the *Bhagavā* without the meaning having been analyzed in detail?’
mn18:20.14: Then this occurred to us, bhante:
mn18:20.15: ‘This venerable Mahākaccāna is praised by the Teacher and esteemed by discerning spiritual companions; venerable Mahākaccāna is capable of analyzing in detail the meaning of this brief statement set out by the *Bhagavā* without the meaning having been analyzed in detail. What if we were to approach venerable Mahākaccāna and ask him about this matter?’
mn18:20.16: Then, bhante, we approached venerable Mahākaccāna, and on arrival we asked him about this matter.
mn18:20.17: And for us, bhante, the meaning was analyzed by venerable Mahākaccāna through these modes, these words, and these expressions.”
mn18:20.2: “What the *Bhagavā* set out for us in a brief statement, bhante, without analyzing the meaning in detail, and then rose from his seat and entered his dwelling:
mn18:20.3: ‘From whatever source, *bhikkhu*, proliferating *saññā*-constructions arise in a person.
mn18:20.4: If there is nothing there to be delighted in, welcomed, or clung to.
mn18:20.5: This is the end of the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayānaṁ*] …
mn18:20.6: it is here that these evil, unwholesome *dhammas* cease without remainder.’
mn18:20.7: Then, bhante, not long after the *Bhagavā* had left, this occurred to us:
mn18:20.8: ‘The *Bhagavā* set out a brief statement for us without analyzing the meaning in detail, and then rose from his seat and entered his dwelling:
mn18:20.9: “From whatever source, *bhikkhu*, proliferating *saññā*-constructions arise in a person.
mn18:21.1: “Mahākaccāna is wise, *bhikkhus*; Mahākaccāna is greatly discerning [*mahāpañño*], *bhikkhus*.
mn18:21.2: If you had asked me about this matter too, *bhikkhus*, I would have explained it in just the same way as it was explained by Mahākaccāna.
mn18:21.3: This is the meaning of it. And that is how you should remember it.”
mn18:22.1: When this was said, venerable Ānanda said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn18:22.2: “Just as, bhante, a man overcome by hunger and weakness might obtain a honeyball, and wherever he would taste it, he would indeed find sweet flavor needing no seasoning.
mn18:22.3: So too, bhante, wherever a *bhikkhu* of capable mind might examine the meaning of this exposition of *dhamma* with discernment [*paññāya*], he would indeed gain gladness, he would indeed gain serenity of mind.
mn18:22.4: What is the name of this exposition of *dhamma*, bhante?”
mn18:22.5: “Therefore, Ānanda, you should remember this exposition of *dhamma* simply as ‘The Honeyball Exposition.’”
mn18:22.7: Venerable Ānanda was glad and approved of what the *Bhagavā* said.
mn18:22.8: The Honeyball is finished, the eighth.
mn18:3.1: And Daṇḍapāṇi the Sakyan, while walking and wandering for exercise, approached the Great Wood.
mn18:3.2: Having plunged into the Great Wood, he approached the bael sapling, where the *Bhagavā* was, and on arrival he exchanged greetings with the *Bhagavā*.
mn18:3.3: After exchanging courteous and memorable talk, he stood to one side, leaning on his staff. Standing to one side, Daṇḍapāṇi the Sakyan said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn18:3.4: “What does the ascetic assert? What does he proclaim?”
mn18:4.1: “Friend, I assert it in such a way that one does not stand disputing with anyone in the world with its devas, Māras, and Brahmās, among this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its devas and humans; and in such a way that *saññās* do not underlie that brahmin dwelling detached from sensual pleasures, free of perplexity, with worry cut off, with *taṇhā* gone for *bhava* and non-*bhava*—
mn18:4.2: that is how I assert it, friend; that is what I proclaim.”
mn18:5.1: When this was said, Daṇḍapāṇi the Sakyan shook his head, stuck out his tongue, made three furrows appear on his forehead, and went away leaning on his staff.
mn18:6.1: Then, in the evening, the *Bhagavā*, having risen from seclusion, approached Nigrodha’s Park and, on arrival, sat down on the seat laid out.
mn18:6.10: When this was said, *bhikkhus*, I said this to Daṇḍapāṇi the Sakyan:
mn18:6.11: ‘Friend, I assert it in such a way that one does not stand disputing with anyone in the world with its devas, Māras, and Brahmās, among this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its devas and humans; and in such a way that *saññās* do not underlie that brahmin dwelling detached from sensual pleasures, free of perplexity, with worry cut off, with *taṇhā* gone for *bhava* and non-*bhava*—
mn18:6.12: that is how I assert it, friend; that is what I proclaim.’
mn18:6.13: When this was said, *bhikkhus*, Daṇḍapāṇi the Sakyan shook his head, stuck out his tongue, made three furrows appear on his forehead, and went away leaning on his staff.”
mn18:6.14: When this was said, a certain *bhikkhu* said this to the *Bhagavā*:
mn18:6.3: “Here, *bhikkhus*, in the morning, having robed up and taken bowl and robe, I entered Kapilavatthu for alms.
mn18:6.4: After walking for alms in Kapilavatthu, after the meal, on returning from the almsround, I approached the Great Wood for the day’s abiding.
mn18:6.5: Having plunged into the Great Wood, I sat down for the day’s abiding at the root of a bael sapling.
mn18:6.6: And Daṇḍapāṇi the Sakyan, *bhikkhus*, while walking and wandering for exercise, approached the Great Wood.
mn18:6.7: Having plunged into the Great Wood, he approached the bael sapling where I was, and on arrival he exchanged greetings with me.
mn18:6.8: After exchanging courteous and memorable talk, he stood to one side, leaning on his staff. Standing to one side, *bhikkhus*, Daṇḍapāṇi the Sakyan said this to me:
mn18:6.9: ‘What does the ascetic assert? What does he proclaim?’
mn18:7.1: “But, bhante, how is it that the *Bhagavā* asserts it in such a way that he does not stand disputing with anyone in the world with its devas, Māras, and Brahmās, among this population with its ascetics and brahmins, its devas and humans?
mn18:7.2: And how is it that *saññās* do not underlie the *Bhagavā*, dwelling detached from sensual pleasures, free of perplexity, with worry cut off, with *taṇhā* gone for *bhava* and non-*bhava*?”
mn18:8.1: “From whatever source, *bhikkhu*, proliferating *saññā*-constructions arise in a person.
mn18:8.2: If there is nothing there to be delighted in, welcomed, or clung to,
mn18:8.3: this is the end of the underlying tendency to lust [*rāgānusayānaṁ*], this is the end of the underlying tendency to aversion [*paṭighānusayānaṁ*], this is the end of the underlying tendency to views [*diṭṭhānusayānaṁ*], this is the end of the underlying tendency to doubt [*vicikicchānusayānaṁ*], this is the end of the underlying tendency to conceit [*mānānusayānaṁ*], this is the end of the underlying tendency to lust for *bhava* [*bhavarāgānusayānaṁ*], this is the end of the underlying tendency to blindness [*avijjānusayānaṁ*], this is the end of taking up rods and weapons, quarrels, conflicts, disputes, mutual accusations, divisive speech, and false speech.
mn18:8.4: It is here that these evil, unwholesome *dhammas* cease without remainder.”
mn18:9.2: Having said this, the Fortunate One rose from his seat and entered his dwelling.
